title: Distributed Variational Quantum Optimisation by Entanglement-Selective Transport
authors: Edric Matwiejew, Pascal Elahi, Ugo Varetto
abstract: Distributed quantum optimisation is challenging because computing the problem cost function across multiple quantum processors requires non-local gates, which can incur overhead in latency and fidelity. Here we introduce QESTO, a distributed variational ansatz for graph-based discrete optimisation that requires only persistent pre-shared Bell pairs for remote operations. Using local operations, it encodes local constraint information in the Bell pairs that is leveraged to produce amplitude transfer towards globally valid distributed solution states. QESTO requires one Bell pair per distributed edge of the problem graph and, after initialisation of the Bell states, uses no non-local gates. On two bounded weighted Wang tile-matching problem ensembles, QESTO achieves stronger convergence to low-cost tilings than equivalently partitioned QAOA with no distributed gates at ansatz depths of two or higher, and exceeds the mean performance of monolithic QAOA at the deepest studied depth in both ensembles. These results suggest that persistent entanglement can support useful variational communication while reducing per-layer non-local gate overhead.
summary: This paper introduces QESTO, a novel variational ansatz designed for distributed quantum optimization on multiple QPUs. It leverages pre-shared Bell pairs to encode and transport constraint information locally, avoiding costly non-local gates. Benchmarking shows QESTO significantly outperforms standard QAOA methods by efficiently concentrating probability amplitude on low-cost solutions.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of performing distributed quantum optimization for graph-based discrete problems when computing the cost function requires non-local gates across multiple quantum processors.
main_result: The proposed QESTO ansatz achieves stronger convergence to low-cost solutions than equivalent partitioned QAOA and outperforms monolithic QAOA on benchmark tile-matching problems, suggesting persistent entanglement is key to efficient distributed computation.
method: QESTO introduces a variational ansatz that uses persistent, pre-shared Bell pairs to encode local constraint information and facilitate amplitude transfer across distributed edges, minimizing the need for non-local gates during the optimization layers.
model_or_system: The model involves quantum computation distributed over multiple Quantum Processing Units (QPUs) solving graph-based discrete optimization problems, such as weighted Wang tile-matching. The core mechanism relies on local operations and shared entanglement (Bell pairs) to simulate long-range interactions.
key_observables: Mean normalized optimality gap (g), mean feasible probability of finding low-cost tilings, and the concentration of probability mass near the optimum solution.
important_parameters: Ansatz depth (p), the number of distributed edges ($|E_{dist}|$), and variational parameters $\{\gamma_r,  eta_r, 	ext{tr}, \eta_r\}$.
main_assumptions: The approach assumes the ability to utilize persistent Bell pairs, which reduces the overhead associated with repeatedly executing non-local gates compared to gate-teleported methods.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the QESTO circuit structure for two QPUs sharing edges, and comparative plots show the mean normalized optimality gap and feasible probability versus ansatz depth for QESTO versus reference QAOA methods.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the problem and the QESTO ansatz, detailing its local and entanglement-based layers. It then benchmarks QESTO against subgraph-QAOA and monolithic QAOA on specific tile-matching ensembles, presenting quantitative results on convergence and probability distribution.
why_interesting: The focus on utilizing persistent entanglement to simulate long-range interactions while minimizing gate overhead is highly relevant for near-term quantum hardware architectures that struggle with high-fidelity, deep non-local gate sequences.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Representative depth-p QESTO circuit for two QPUs sharing two distributed edges e1, e2 ∈Edist. Each QPU hosts a local problem register Li and one communication qubit cek,i per incident distributed edge, with one Bell pair prepared across each edge. One ansatz layer applies local cost and mixing unitaries, followed by label encoding, transport, and decoding, as in (12).

Fig. 2. Example bounded weighted Wang tile-matching instance from the 2×4 ensemble. The top-left panel shows the tile catalogue, the top-right panel shows a globally optimal solution, and the bottom panel gives the position- dependent tile-assignment costs, with each tuple ordered clockwise from the upper-left catalogue tile. Blue and grey wedges encode the binary edge labels. The vertical red dashed line marks the inter-QPU boundary for optimisation by QESTO and subgraph-QAOA.

Fig. 3. QESTO vs. subgraph-QAOA and monolithic QAOA on the 2×2 (top) and 2×4 (bottom) layouts. The left panels show the mean normalised optimality gap g (see (14)) vs. depth p, and the right panels show the mean feasible probability vs. p; shaded bands are ±1 SEM across instances. All points use 20 instances.

Fig. 4. Mean cost-rank distributions for the 2×2 ensemble at depth p = 5 (top) and the 2×4 ensemble at depth p = 8 (bottom). Rank 0 denotes the globally optimal cost level, and higher ranks denote successively larger distinct classical cost values. Curves show the mean probability mass across instances; shaded bands are ±1 SEM. The black dotted line shows the distribution obtained by uniform random sampling of the solution space. All points use 20 instances.
